sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Invasive Group A Streptococcus (iGAS) is caused by *Streptococcus pyogenes*, a bacterium capable of causing a spectrum of disease from superficial infections to rapidly life-threatening invasive conditions including necrotising fasciitis and streptococcal toxic shock syndrome (STSS).

**Incubation:** Typically 1–3 days (up to 30 days)

**Infectious period:** From 7 days before symptom onset until 24 hours after effective antibiotic therapy commences

**Notifiable disease in Australia.** iGAS is associated with high mortality — early recognition and rapid transport are critical.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
### Invasive Presentations
- **Necrotising fasciitis:** Rapidly spreading skin infection — red, warm, swollen, tender; **pain disproportionate to appearance**; skin may become dusky/blistered; crepitus (gas in tissue) is a late sign
- **Streptococcal Toxic Shock Syndrome (STSS):** Fever, hypotension, multi-organ failure
- **Bacteraemia/Sepsis:** Fever, tachycardia, hypotension
- **Pneumonia:** Pleuritic chest pain, fever, dyspnoea

### General Signs & Symptoms
- Fever and/or chills
- Shortness of breath, chest pain
- Rapidly spreading skin infection with possible pus or ulceration
- Signs of sepsis or shock with multi-organ failure
- Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
- Dizziness, headache, neck stiffness

### Paediatric Presentation
Fever, rash, mottled limbs, headache, limb/abdominal pain, poor feeding, reduced urine output, lethargy, seizures, increased work of breathing, persistent tachycardia

### Puerperal (Postpartum) Presentation
Vaginal bleeding or purulent discharge, fever, lower abdominal pain — evaluate mother-infant pair

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
### High-Risk Features — Treat as Time Critical
- Pain out of proportion to visible injury
- Rapid skin changes (dusky, purple, blistering)
- Sepsis Red Flags (refer Sepsis CPG)
- Haemodynamic instability

### Transmission
- **Contact:** Direct contact with nose/throat secretions or infected skin lesions
- **Droplet:** Respiratory droplets and aerosols

### Mine Site Context
- Skin injuries (lacerations, abrasions) common on mine sites — any wound with rapidly worsening pain, spreading erythema, or systemic deterioration should raise concern for iGAS
- Low threshold for urgent evacuation: iGAS can progress from minor wound to necrotising fasciitis within hours
- Notify site medical officer of suspected cases

### PPE
- Gloves
- P2/N95 respirator
- Eye protection
- Gown/coveralls (risk-assess; required for significant wound contact)
- Apply surgical mask to patient if tolerated

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Maintain low threshold for **Emergency Department transport** — do not delay for definitive wound management in the field
- Administer oxygen if hypoxaemic
- Apply clean dressings; do not debride or incise
- Use NEWS2 or sepsis criteria to grade urgency
- **Sepsis with iGAS features = time-critical transport**

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- IV access; fluid resuscitation per Sepsis/Hypovolaemic Shock CPG
- Manage fever and pain as appropriate
- Serial vital signs and GCS monitoring during transport
- Notify receiving hospital of suspected iGAS/necrotising fasciitis **before arrival** — surgical team activation may be required
- Do not attempt field incision/drainage of suspected necrotising fasciitis

### Septic Shock
Manage per Sepsis CPG — target SBP ≥90 mmHg, titrate fluids, vasopressor support (hospital-initiated)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- **Notifiable disease** — report to WA Department of Health
- Definitive treatment is IV antibiotics (penicillin + clindamycin) and surgical debridement for necrotising fasciitis — time from presentation to surgery directly impacts survival
- Close contacts (especially household/accommodation contacts) should be assessed for prophylaxis by public health
- Document all patient contacts who may require follow-up
